Audit & Planning (2 skills)

column3: The 5 rules that get you to $1M will kill you at $10M.
column12:
The 5 rules that get you to $1M will kill you at $10M.
Here are 62 GTM rules set for each one:

→ $0-1M ARR · Founder-led survival (5 rules)
↳ One ICP, defined by pain.
↳ The founder sends every cold email manually.
↳ Founder posts 2x/week on LinkedIn. No ads, no SDRs, no/little automation.
↳ Revenue closed is the only metric.

Tools: Scripe, Taplio, AuthoredUp, Claude , OpenAI HubSpot , Attio

→ $1-5M ARR · Repeatability (10 rules)
↳ ICP scored by signal density.
↳ Domain warmed 4 weeks, 3-5 inboxes per domain.
↳ First AE closes most deals. SDR books, the founder still backs up enterprise + complex deals.

Tools: HubSpot + Clay + Instantly + 1 signal source.

→ $5-10M ARR · Scaling (12 rules)
↳ ICP tiered A/B/C. 2-3 SDRs + 1 manager.
↳ LinkedIn Ads $8-15k/month (≈1-2% of ARR) + BOFU/case studies.

→ $10-25M ARR · Operating leverage (15 rules)

column3: Here are 15 cold email frameworks mapped directly to 3 awareness levels to use in Claude Code:
column12:
Here are 15 cold email frameworks mapped directly to 3 awareness levels to use in Claude Code:

The biggest cold email mistake isn't a bad framework.

It's using it on the wrong buyer.

Eugene Schwartz figured this out in 1966.
15 cold email frameworks map directly to 3 awareness levels.
Most people pick one they like and run it on everyone.

Here is which one to use and when:

Problem Aware → they know something's wrong, don't know solutions exist

↳ Josh Braun "Write a Good Cold Email" → name the exact problem, don't pitch, don't explain
↳  Vin Matano 🐝 "Leader Responsibilities" → make them curious about their own role, no solution mentioned
↳ Patrick Trümpi "Challenge of Similar Companies" → show them they're not alone with this problem
↳ Keenan (Gap Selling) → show the gap between where they are and where they need to be
↳ ☠️ Belal Batrawy "Mic Drop Method" → permission, problem, provoke, promise

Solution Aware → they're comparing options, probably talking to 3 other people right now

↳ Thibaut Souyris "Do the Math" → put numbers to it, let them run the calculation
↳ Chelsea Castle "Neutral Insight" → hand them a 3rd party stat that reframes the conversation
↳ Jordan Crawford "Upfront Value" → give something useful before you ask for anything
↳ Matt Dixon (Challenger Sale) → teach them something they didn't know, then connect it to you
↳ Todd Caponi "The Transparency Sale" → open with your biggest weakness, trust builds faster than you think

Most Aware → they've seen you, they know you, just haven’t made the next step

↳ Guillermo Blanco "Short Trigger" → 30 words max, name the exact signal, nothing else
↳ 💜 🔮 Will Allred "Ask Before Pitch" → one direct question
↳ Bow Tied Sales Guy "Not Too Different Persona" → you're just like them, peer framing
↳ Chris Voss (Never Split the Difference) → one open question, empathy, and stop there
↳ Becc Holland "Flip the Script" → say out loud what they're already thinking about you

My tip for you:

Don't pick your favourite framework.
Pick the framework that matches where the buyer is.

Which tier does your list sit in right now?

column3: $1M+ pipeline from LinkedIn doesn't come from posting.
column12:
$1M+ pipeline from LinkedIn doesn't come from posting.

It comes from turning content into a signal engine.

Most B2B teams treat LinkedIn like a publishing platform.

The best GTM teams treat it like a demand intelligence layer.

Here’s the system we run across our own team and GTM clients:

OLD WAY

↳ random founder posts
↳ company page updates
↳ likes and comments disappear
↳ no connection to CRM
↳ no signal capture
↳ engagement never becomes pipeline

NEW WAY

↳ employee‑led + founder‑led content
↳ signals captured across the buying committee
↳ enrichment and scoring in Clay
↳ routing into HubSpot
↳ outbound triggered while intent is fresh

Here’s how the system actually works.

Step 1 → Create operator‑level content

↳ tactical frameworks from real GTM work
↳ breakdowns of systems and workflows
↳ customer stories and experiments
↳ comment‑gated resources and templates

Tools we use
↳ Claude
↳  Scripe 
↳ Apify 
↳ Figma 

The goal is not engagement.
The goal is high‑intent signals.

Step 2 → Capture every signal

When someone engages, we track it.

↳ profile views
↳ post engagement
↳ website visits
↳ pricing page visits
↳ buying committee activity

Tools

↳ Clay
↳ RB2B
↳  Vector 👻 
↳ Trigify
↳ PhantomBuster

All signals flow into Clay.
Qualified activity syncs into HubSpot 

Step 3 → Enrich and score the account

Now the system understands the account.

↳ ICP match
↳ role and seniority
↳ company tier
↳ signal velocity
↳ buying committee coverage

Then we route by priority.

Tier 1

↳ AE outreach immediately
↳ tools: Nooks/Orum, lemlist 

Tier 2

↳ multichannel outbound
↳ tools: Lemlist, Expandi, Instantly

Tier 3

↳ programmatic sequences via EmailBison

This is how we can turn content into a qualified pipeline.

Step 4 → Convert warm demand

Once the signals hit the threshold:

↳ outbound triggers from HubSpot
↳ LinkedIn outreach based on insight
↳ ads retarget engaged accounts 
↳ meetings booked while intent is still fresh

LinkedIn content is not the engine.
The engine is: content + signals + scoring + routing.

Comment ENGINE if you want the full workflow map.

P.S. Most teams try to generate a pipeline from cold lists. We help GTM teams generate it from signals.

column3: This is what an AI-native GTM engine looks like when it is fully running.
column12:
This is what an AI-native GTM engine looks like when it is fully running.

3 AI agents. Campaigns 24/7. 4 layers. Zero manual work.

Everything repetitive is automated. Everything that needs judgment stays human.

Here's exactly how it works:

Step 1: The Prospecting Engine with one source of truth

↳ HubSpot has every contact and company fully enriched
↳ Accounts sourced, enriched, and tiered automatically
↳ Contacts scored by decision power — influencer, champion, or buyer
↳ Every account has a defined next step based on where they are in the journey
↳ Runs centrally through Claude Code and HubSpot CRM

Step 2: Inbox health scoring with domain blacklist monitoring

↳ EmailGuard runs scheduled inbox placement tests automatically
↳ Every domain is checked every 24 hours for blacklist listings
↳ n8n + Airtable reads the placement results and tags each inbox
↳ Airtable dashboard updates in real time (all clients, all inboxes, one place)
↳ Slack sends an automated report on every client, so nothing gets missed for our GTM engineering team

Step 3: Automatically adjust the daily cold email and warm-up limits

↳ N8N listens to EmailGuard results and moves limits up or down
↳ Cold volume and warm volume adjust separately, per inbox
↳ No one touches a setting manually

Step 4: The right email to the right account

↳ High-fit + high engagement accounts go into a direct response campaign
↳ High-fit but no engagement yet, go into a nurture sequence
↳ Low-fit or unassigned accounts get pushed by Clay into LinkedIn, Google, and Meta ad audiences

One system for deliverability. One system for prospecting. Both talk to each other.

The result:

Most teams find out their inbox health is damaged after reply rates drop.
We find out before the email goes out.

How do you currently know when an inbox is in trouble? Are you doing this fully manually?

column3: 7 questions. Every discovery call. Real-time AI scoring after every one. Here’s the exact framework:
column12:
7 questions. Every discovery call. Real-time AI scoring after every one. Here’s the exact framework:

The 7 questions we ask every prospect:

Question 1: "What made you reach out right now?"
↳ By the time they call, they've already done 70% of the research. If they can not answer this in one sentence, they are not ready.

Question 2: "What does your current go-to-market motion look like?"
↳ You need to know what is running and how it is working and set up before you propose anything.

Question 3: "What has not worked?"
↳ They will tell you exactly what not to sell them.

Question 4: "Who else is involved in this decision?"
↳ A deal that stalls always stalls because of someone you never met.

Question 5: "What does success look like in 90 days?"
↳ Specific. A number if possible. This becomes your proposal framing.

Question 6: "What happens if this does not get solved this quarter?"
↳ Tells you how much urgency they actually feel, not how much they say they feel.

Question 7: "Is there anything that would stop this from moving forward?"
↳ Every unspoken objection lives in the answer to this question.

What happens when the call ends:

Fellow - AI Meeting Assistant transcribes the full call automatically. (this can be Gong , Otter)
Claude and Clay read the transcript and scored it across four dimensions:

↳ ICP fit: firmographic + pain match
↳ Urgency signal: Is this quarter or next quarter?
↳ Stakeholder map: who is in the room and who is missing?
↳ Package fit: Which GT service matches what they described

The output: a one-page brief. Package recommendation. Next step. Handed to sales as a review before the follow-up goes out, and for sales coaching purposes.

No manual note-taking. No "I think they need X." Data from what they actually said.

We also built three standalone use cases for teams that do not need the full GTM engineering service:

↳ Inbound Lead Enrichment: Every inbound lead is scored 1-10 before anyone calls them
↳ Discovery Call Preparation: Claude pulls a one-page company brief from Clay before every call
↳ CSM Calls Proactive Scoring: flags at-risk accounts before the CSM knows there is a problem

Comment "CALL" and I will send you the discovery framework: the 7 questions, the Fellow AI setup, and the Claude scoring prompt we use.

column3: Cold email reply rate in the US: 3.43%. LinkedIn reply rate in Europe: 11.81%.
column12:
Cold email reply rate in the US: 3.43%. LinkedIn reply rate in Europe: 11.81%.

Here is what actually works in each market:

Same tool. Same sequence. Different market.

Different market means different strategies.

Here is how to approach it and save a lot of trial and error when you expand:

United Kingdom
→ Most competitive B2B market in Europe
↳ 82% of B2B teams are increasing LinkedIn spend right now
↳ Efficiency-led messaging wins. Short, direct, value first.
↳ Omnichannel required. Cold email alone is not enough.

Netherlands
→ 53% LinkedIn penetration (highest in Europe)
↳ Cold email to personal addresses is illegal under GDPR. Business emails only.
↳ Consensus buying: plan for 3 to 5 stakeholders from day one.
↳ Build visibility on LinkedIn before you reach out. Content first.

Germany
→ Lowest email engagement in Europe (3.3% CTR)
↳ Technical proof before any call. Case studies, documentation, certifications.
↳ Emotional copy does not land. Credibility over personality, every time.
↳ Long cycles are normal. Budget for them.

France
→ Relationship-first, high-context market
↳ Trust before pitch. Always.
↳ Peer referral outperforms cold outreach here consistently.
↳ Build the relationship before you ask for anything.

Spain and Italy
→ Highest LinkedIn reply rate globally at 11.81%
↳ WhatsApp for B2B follow-up works. 50%+ response rate.
↳ Visual and emotional storytelling outperforms data-heavy copy.
↳ Warm beats cold by a large margin.

Tools to use:
↳ Multichannel: lemlist Jason AI (Reply)
↳ LinkedIn: Expandi, Heyreach, La Growth Machine, DuxSoup, Dripify
↳ Email: Woodpecker.co, Instantly.ai, Smartlead, Clay

Nadja Komnenic ex-sales from Lemlist and working with our Heyreach friends and sees US + EU sales, so it's worth following her, alongside with Nikola Siljanoski for Linkedin best practices.

Every market runs differently.

Don’t the US move into European markets and vice versa?

column3: B2B GTM in 2026 looks nothing like 2021.
column12:
B2B GTM in 2026 looks nothing like 2021.

In 2021: SDRs first, data later. In 2026: signals first, SDRs last (most of them are enabled with AI).

Here is the new 5-layer order:

1. Build out the foundation
↳ Say your ICP pain out loud: repeat their pain in their own words
↳ Clarify your offer: Scaling a weak offer produces a lot of noise and no recurring revenue
↳ Name your enemy: give buyers something to remember you against
↳ Stack signals: funding round + GTM hire + engaged your content = one outreach trigger
↳ Protect the inbox: SPF, DKIM, DMARC, domain warm-up, copy never gets read from spam

Tools we usually connect here:
Clay, Apify, Trigify, Common Room, Cognism , Demandbase , Warmly, 

2. Strategize
↳ Let triggers write the copy: company hires a GTM engineer? That IS the email
↳ Run multiple channels: Content builds awareness. Outbound activates intent. Ads reinforce both.
↳ Narrow down your ICP: “B2B SaaS ” is a category vs. ”Series A SaaS with an existing inbound team” is a real target.
3. Execute

↳ Content: Builds the audience before outreach ever starts.
↳ Signal‑based outbound: Reach out when something actually happened.
↳ Retargeting Ads should follow engagement, not random targeting.

Tools here usually look like:
lemlist, EmailBison, Expandi, LinkedIn Ads, ZenABM , Unify, Cargo, Default

We systemize these to make sure they compound.

4. Amplify
↳ Boost posts that already booked meetings, don't guess what to pay to promote
↳ Repurpose your best-performing content into content-led outbound sequences
↳ Run lookalike campaigns against your closed-won accounts

Stack we use:
Dock, HubSpot , Fellow AI

5. Scale
↳ Systematize what worked: document the triggers, the copy, the list criteria
↳ Add channels only after layers 1 and 2 are solid
↳ Replace manual steps with agents once the process proves out

Pipeline compounds when the foundation is right.
Most teams are building from layer 3 up (activation). That’s why it doesn’t work:

You can’t build the roof on the house if the floors are missing:

Foundation = data.
Architecture = signals + enrichment.
Activation = manual (SDRs, AEs) + AI SDRs.
GTM Copilot = the final layer, once the system runs itself

A few other experts whom you can follow + learn from:
1) Chris Walker: modern GTM + signal-driven marketing
2) Sangram Vajre: GTM frameworks
3) Guillaume Moubeche: besides  lemlist , a lot of tips on bootstrapping from 0 to $20M+
4) and ofc: Devin Reed: content-led GTM!!

column3: Finding and enriching leads used to take a full day.
column12:
Finding and enriching leads used to take a full day.
Prospeo + Claude Code does it in minutes.

You can use it to find companies, score contacts, enrich emails, verify phones, and launch campaigns:

Here's the exact system step by step:

1️⃣ Find companies
Pull from Prospeo's Search API.

- Location, size, industry, keywords
- 30+ filters to get exactly who you want
Claude reads your GT-Outbound.md and calls the API automatically. No manual setup.

2️⃣ Score and tier contacts
Prospeo's Find People API: 300,000+ contacts, refreshed every 7 days.
Claude runs your scoring-criteria.md against the raw data.

- Tier 1 → 18% reply rate (multiple signals 4-6 together. <200 accounts)
- Tier 2 → 6–8% (1st-party data, engaged, warmed up prospects)
- Tier 3 → 1-2%% (full cold audience)
Claude does this by using our deterministic scoring. No hallucination. Fully auditable.

3️⃣ Enrich emails
Prospeo Email Finder API.

- 98% accuracy
- 5-step verification: catch-all handling, spam trap removal, honeypot filtering
- Only charged for valid results
Clay tested 8 providers across 4,000+ contacts. Prospeo ranked #1 for accuracy.

4️⃣ Enrich phones
Prospeo Phone Finder API.

- 125M+ verified mobile numbers
- 30% pickup rate vs 12% Apollo average
Claude understands the signal context and writes a different copy angle per persona.

5️⃣ Deploy the campaign

- EmailBison via API.
- Claude creates the campaign and uploads the leads by itself.
Then writes the results back to memory.md. Every next campaign can auto-improve itself.

The result:

- Bounce rate: 15% → 2%

column3: If you lead revenue, read this:
column12:
If you lead revenue, read this:
AI didn’t change GTM. 
It changed how you acquire businesses.

The last 10 years of GTM were about adding tools.

CRM.
Outbound tools.
Enrichment.
Intent data.
Automation.

But the real shift is happening in the people running them.

The role that used to win GTM is disappearing.

And a new one is replacing it.

You can see it clearly if you map the stages of GTM maturity.

Stage 1: Founder seller
↳ The founder runs sales directly
↳ Prospecting, messaging, closing
↳ Everything is manual

Typical tools
↳ LinkedIn
↳ Gmail
↳ Calendly

Stage 2: Marketing + SDR motion
↳ Marketing generates demand
↳ SDRs run outbound
↳ Sales closes

Typical stack
↳ HubSpot
↳ Apollo
↳ lemlist 
↳ Sales Navigator

This is where most B2B teams struggle.

More tools.
But no orchestration.

Stage 3: Full GTM team
↳ Demand gen
↳ SDR team
↳ RevOps
↳ Sales

Channels start reinforcing each other.

Typical motion
↳ LinkedIn content
↳ outbound
↳ paid ads

Typical tools
↳ HubSpot
↳ LinkedIn Ads
↳ Lemlist / Instantly
↳ ZenABM 

Now the system starts to scale.

Stage 4: RevOps orchestration
↳ Systems connect the stack
↳ CRM, enrichment, routing, attribution
↳ Processes become repeatable

Signals start driving prioritization.

Typical tools
↳ Clay
↳ Common Room
↳ RB2B
↳ HubSpot workflows
↳ n8n / Integromat 

This was the peak of GTM sophistication for years.

But a new stage just appeared.

Stage 5: AI‑native GTM operators
↳ One operator designs the system
↳ AI handles research, enrichment, scoring, and routing
↳ Humans focus on strategy, messaging, and deals

Typical stack
↳ Claude Code
↳ AI agents
↳ Clay
↳ n8n
↳ HubSpot 

This is where GTM engineers come in.
They don’t just run campaigns.

They build the systems.
The best ones today combine

↳ RevOps thinking

↳ Content and messaging fundamentals

A role that didn’t really exist a few years ago.

column3: We get 50% of our clients through LinkedIn.
column12:
We get 50% of our clients through LinkedIn.
Here’s how to capture them and start a conversation with them:

Most prospects don’t book immediately.
They read, listen, and engage first.

At Growth Today, we see it every week with new clients: the warm signals are there.

The way to achieve the same is simple.
Here's the 5-step framework:

1. Capture across channels (not just LinkedIn)
↳ LinkedIn: profile views, comments, likes, connection requests
↳ Website: anonymous visitors ( Snitcher  ), pricing page hits, competitor comparison visits
↳ Content: who's reading your stuff (Trigify / Expandi / Vector 👻  / CommonRoom)
↳ Account-level: job changes in the buying committee (Clay / Champify )
↳ Email: opens, clicks, repeat reads in your sequencer

2. Stack the signals (if you have a very strong inbound motion, otherwise skip)
↳ 1 signal = reason to reach out
↳ 3+ signals from the same account = prioritize
↳ Multiple people from the same company = absolutely prioritize
↳ Track signal velocity (how fast multiple signals appear on one account)

3. Qualify against ICP
↳ Role + seniority match
↳ Company fit (size, industry, stage)
↳ Buying authority + buying committee structure

4. Reference the insight (not the signal)
↳ Reference the specific insight (if make sense)
↳ Don't pretend you don't know they engaged
↳ Keep it short, contextual, and low-pressure
↳ Multi-thread the account (if the top account + multiple engagements have happened)

5. Track the right metrics
↳ Chat-to-response and response to meeting conversion rate
↳ Signal velocity (signal → meeting time)
↳ Signal source mix (LinkedIn vs website vs content vs email)
↳ Account-level pipeline coverage from signals

Familiarity drives the B2B pipeline.
Cold lists alone don't.

♻ Repost so more B2B teams stop ignoring warm signals.

P.S. We build these outbound + content systems for $1M+ ARR B2B GTM teams.

column3: 5 funding alerts ≠ signal-based ABM.
column12:
5 funding alerts ≠ signal-based ABM.
Here's how to run ABM that actually works:

If you added Crunchbase funding alerts to your list, that's a trigger. Not a signal.

→ Triggers are public events.
Series A closed. New VP Sales hired. Installed competitor.

→ Signals are private behaviors from the buying committee.
3 buyers from one account on /pricing this week. Champion engaged 3x with your LinkedIn this week. VP of Marketing downloaded the content.

The problem with triggers: they're public.

Every SDR with a Crunchbase login can pull the same Series B list Monday morning.

By Tuesday afternoon, 40 SDRs sent the same "congrats on the raise" email.

Your prospect ignores all of them.

→ Trigger window dies in 14 days. After that, you're another cold email.

Signals are different. Plenty of them, if you connect the right sources.

→ Signal window dies in 24-48 hours. If you don't use it in time, they might book a demo with someone else.

Most teams never get past triggers.

Only 25% of B2B teams use real intent data. The other 75% are still sending "congrats on the raise" emails.

Here's how we run ABM at Growth Today:

1 trigger = the reason to reach out.
3 signals = proof they're considering it right now. (or have a need + urgency)

One Tier 1 account, properly stacked:

→ New VP Sales hired (trigger)
→ 3 buyers from the company on /pricing this week (signal)
→ Champion engaged 3x with your LinkedIn this week (signal)
→ Marketing engaged through retargeting ads (signal)

Four reasons to send an email. Each is strong on its own.

Reply rates: 5-12% on a stacked signal account. 1-3% on a trigger-only account.

The difference: your messaging reads like you've been paying attention. Not like you ran a Crunchbase export.

Here's where most ABM programs break:

Sales chases the 5 trigger accounts. Marketing has no idea that those are the priority.
The other 95 accounts on the Tier 1 list get nothing.

→ No content
→ No ads
→ No retargeting

When a buyer from one of those 95 finally gives you intent data, nobody sees it.

The fix:

Sales and Marketing run the same Tier 1 list together.

→ Marketing covers all 95 with content, ads, and retargeting.
→ Sales puts higher effort on the trigger-stacked accounts.

That's signal-based ABM.

Not 5 funding alerts on the same account in a CRM.

Comment "INTENT" and I'll send you 63+ curated triggers and signals you can slice and dice for your stack.

P.S. We build AI-native ABM programs for B2B teams at Growth Today. Triggers and signals are 2 of 30+ data points we work with. Book a time if you run ABM, but it's not connected, and you want to stop wasting money.

column3: I have spent 825+ hours inside Claude Code. Here’s the cheat sheet our whole GTM team uses:
column12:
I have spent 825+ hours inside Claude Code. Here’s the cheat sheet our whole GTM team uses:

Save this cheat sheet. It will save you weeks of trial and error. 👇

1️⃣ Install in 30 seconds
↳ claude.ai/install or `brew install --cask claude-code`
↳ Run claude /init immediately after install.

2️⃣ CLAUDE.md → your operating system
Without it: generic AI.
With it: expert in your stack, your rules, your workflows, and what’s your role in the organization (saved in the memory)

↳ `./CLAUDE.md` → each repo
↳ `~/.claude/CLAUDE.md` → global rules
↳ `.claude/rules/` → path-specific rules

Keep it under 200 lines. Clear. Direct. Specific.

3️⃣ Skills → reusable knowledge

Claude reads the description and decides which skill to use. You don't call them manually or using the / unless you want to enforce it.

We have 50+ skills at Growth Today:
↳ GTM playbook generation
↳ Signal-based outbound frameworks
↳ Data enrichment workflows with Clay
↳ Campaign copy and design briefs
↳ Client onboarding
↳ Email infrastructure setup through N8N automation

Skills handle knowledge. MCP handles actions.

4️⃣ MCP Servers → your app store

↳ Claude mcp add --transport http [name] [url]`
↳ HubSpot , Gmail, Slack, Sheets, databases (will cover 80% of your GTM use cases)
↳ Scope by: local, project, or user

5️⃣ Hooks → fires on events

↳ PreToolUse → block risky commands
↳ PostToolUse → auto-format code
↳ SessionStart → reload your context
↳ Notification → desktop alerts

We use N8N for automated inbox placement test and adjust the daily cold + warmup settings based on the recent email health results.

8️⃣ Useful Slash commands
↳ `/init` → build CLAUDE.md from your existing code
↳ `/compact` → clear bloated context
↳ `/memory` → check what Claude remembers
↳ `/cost` → track token spend live
↳ `/review` → pull request review

7️⃣ Session workflow
↳ `claude -n "feature-name"` → start fresh
↳ `claude -c` → continue last session
↳ `claude -r "project-name"` → jump back in
↳ `claude --fork-session` → branch your work

Tip: One session per feature. Commit to Git before you switch.

9️⃣ CLI flags for headless and automation mode
↳ `claude -p "query"` → one-shot runs, plug into scripts and CI
↳ `claude --add-dir ../other-repo` → merge context from another repo
↳ `claude --effort high` → deep thinking mode
↳ `claude --max-budget-usd 5` → hard spend cap
↳ `claude --permission-mode plan` → read-only, no edits

We use headless mode to run our prospecting engine and ship mini-tools that capture leads. 50+ skills handle everything from GTM playbook generation to client onboarding.

Setup takes 30 minutes. The value of using skills is there every day.

P.S If you want to save this cheat sheet, comment “Claude cheat sheet,” and I’ll send this to you.
